1: A Levite man marries a Levite woman — the deliverer will come from the priestly tribe. The NT notes that Jesus belongs to the royal tribe (Judah/David) through Joseph, but Luke 1:36 identifies Elizabeth (mother of John the Baptist) as a daughter of Aaron, and Elizabeth and Mary are relatives — meaning Jesus has both royal and priestly lineage in his extended family. The priestly and royal vocations that were constitutionally separate in the Mosaic order converge in Christ (Hebrews 7:14-16).
2: She saw that he was good (ki tov) — the creation goodness formula (Genesis 1:4, 10, 12, etc.) applied to the child. A shadow: the Incarnation is the arrival of the one who is the image of God (2 Corinthians 4:4; Colossians 1:15) — the one in whom the goodness of creation is fully expressed and from whom it flows. Joseph and Mary hide the child from Herod for two years (Matthew 2:13-15) just as the mother hides Moses for three months — the preservation of the deliverer by those who recognize his significance.
3: The tevah (basket/ark) placed in the Nile waters — a type with two layers. First, the tevah (used only here and in Gen 6-9 for Noah's ark) signals that Moses is a new Noah: preserved in a vessel on threatening waters while death surrounds. Second, the infant placed in the waters who emerges as deliverer anticipates the Baptism pattern — Jesus enters the Jordan's waters (Matthew 3:13-17), is identified as God's Son, and emerges to lead his people through the wilderness. Both Noah's ark and the baptismal waters accomplish preservation through death-threatening water.
4: Miriam watches from a distance — the faithful witness at the water, positioned at the margin of events. A shadow: women watch Jesus from a distance at the cross (Matthew 27:55-56), and women are the first witnesses at the empty tomb (Matthew 28:1-10). The marginal witness — female, young, powerless — is consistently the first and most faithful observer of the deliverer's vulnerability and triumph throughout redemptive history.
5: Pharaoh's daughter descends to the Nile to bathe and discovers the basket — providence arranges the meeting of the condemned child with the one who has power to save him. A revelation of God: YHWH uses the unexpected, the Gentile, the powerful-for-worldly-purposes to accomplish his purpose. The Magi (Gentile astronomers) come to honor Jesus; the Roman centurion confesses him at the cross (Matthew 27:54). Those outside the covenant people often recognize and protect the deliverer.
6: She had compassion on him — vatachmol, from the same root as racham (womb-love, compassion). A revelation of God: the character of divine compassion that moves YHWH to deliver Israel (Ex Exodus 3:7: 'I know their sufferings') is expressed through a human being who sees, recognizes, and has compassion on a condemned child. Jesus is consistently described as moved with compassion (esplagchnisthe — Matthew 9:36, Exodus 14:14, Exodus 15:32, Exodus 20:34), seeing what others pass by. The compassion of Pharaoh's daughter is a creaturely reflection of the divine compassion that became incarnate in Christ.
7: Miriam's initiative — arranging for Moses' own mother to nurse him at Pharaoh's expense. A shadow: the providential reversal of the death decree through human initiative and divine orchestration. The Incarnation is the ultimate such reversal: what the powers of this world intended as defeat (the cross) becomes the means of cosmic victory. The deliverer is preserved and nourished by the very household that sought his death.
8: Pharaoh's daughter accepts Miriam's offer — the authority that condemned now sponsors. A shadow: the Roman court that condemned Jesus (crucifixion under Pontius Pilate, a Roman legal execution) has its verdict reversed when a Roman soldier confesses him as the Son of God (Matthew 27:54) and the empire eventually adopts the faith. The household of the oppressor becomes the context in which the deliverer is formed — Joseph's imprisonment by Potiphar becomes his path to Pharaoh's court; Jesus' death under Roman authority becomes the basis of the Roman church.
9: 'Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages' — the mother of the condemned child is paid by the household of condemnation to raise her son. A revelation of God: divine irony as the mode of grace. What the world intends as final becomes the condition of formation. The wisdom of God is foolishness to the world (1 Corinthians 1:25); the power of God is displayed in what appears weak. Jochebed, paid by Pharaoh's court to nurse the future liberator of Israel, embodies this principle at the personal level.
10: Moses is raised in the Egyptian palace — forty years of formation in the household of the oppressor. Acts 7:22 adds: 'Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians and was powerful in speech and action.' A shadow: Jesus grows up hidden in Nazareth, his messianic identity concealed while he is formed through ordinary human life, labor, and learning (Luke 2:52: he grew in wisdom and stature). The deliverer's public identity is preceded by decades of hidden formation that the world neither sees nor values.
11: Moses goes out to see his brothers' suffering and identifies with them — the moment of identification. A type: the Incarnation is this moment raised to cosmic scale. John 1:14: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.' Jesus goes out to where his people are, takes on their condition (Philippians 2:6-8: 'being found in human form, he humbled himself'). Moses identifies with the Hebrews by going where they are; the eternal Son identifies with humanity by becoming what we are.
12: Moses kills the Egyptian and hides him in the sand — the failed first rescue, the unauthorized act of justice. A shadow of the contrast with Christ: Moses acts without commission, in secret, and the result is flight rather than liberation. Jesus acts with full divine authorization, openly, and the result is completed redemption. Moses' failed attempt highlights what is distinctive about Christ's: not impulsive but sent; not hidden but public; not partial but complete (Hebrews 3:3-6: Jesus worthy of more glory than Moses).
13: Moses is rejected by a Hebrew who asks 'Who made you a ruler and judge over us?' — a direct citation by Stephen in Acts 7:27, 35 as the pattern repeated with Jesus. Stephen explicitly argues: this is how Israel treats its sent deliverers. The one raised up by God to rescue them is first rejected by the very people he was sent to save; only after rejection, suffering, and divine vindication is he recognized as the appointed deliverer. Stephen uses this argument to explain the crucifixion: Israel rejected Jesus as they rejected Moses at first.
14: A type, explicitly drawn in Acts 7:27, 35: 'Who made you ruler and judge?' — the rejection of the sent deliverer by those he was sent to save. Stephen's argument in Acts 7:25-27 is that Moses is the prototype of the rejected and then vindicated deliverer: 'He assumed that his own people would understand that God was using him to rescue them, but they did not.' The NT uses this rejection-then-vindication pattern as the interpretive framework for the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus.
15: Moses flees to Midian and spends forty years as a shepherd in the wilderness. A shadow: the forty years in Midian correspond structurally to Jesus' forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11). Both involve withdrawal, testing, and formation before the public commissioning that inaugurates the mission of deliverance. Both figures emerge from the wilderness experience to confront the powers of their day. The wilderness is the school of the deliverer — the place where human resources are stripped away and divine provision is the only ground to stand on.
16: Moses meets the seven daughters of Jethro at the well in Midian — the shepherd who defends the vulnerable at the place of water. A shadow: Jesus meets the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well (John 4:6-26), offering living water and identifying himself as the promised Messiah. The well-meeting scene is a patriarchal type-scene (Isaac/Rebekah, Jacob/Rachel) that culminates in Jesus' conversation at the well — the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) meets the excluded at the water.
17: Moses drives away the male shepherds and waters the flock for Jethro's daughters — the shepherd protects the vulnerable against exploitation. A type: Jesus drives out those who exploit the temple (Matthew 21:12-13), cleansing the place of divine encounter from commercial predation. The Good Shepherd protects the sheep from the hireling who abandons them (John 10:11-13). Moses' protective act at the Midianite well is a miniature of the Good Shepherd's defining character.
18: Jethro's daughters report 'an Egyptian man delivered us' — the deliverer's identity is hidden behind his appearance. Moses looks Egyptian; he is Hebrew. A shadow: Jesus is identified as a Galilean carpenter (Mark 6:3), his divine identity hidden behind human appearance. Those who receive him are those who look past the apparent and recognize the real (Matthew 16:16-17: Peter's confession — 'You are the Messiah' — revealed by the Father, not by flesh and blood).
19: The deliverer rescues the daughters from hostile shepherds and provides water for their flock. A revelation of God: the same divine character that acts on behalf of the powerless against the powerful — the theme of Ex Exodus 3:7-10 — is expressed in Moses' act at the well. The God who hears the cry of the oppressed in Egypt acts through Moses at a Midianite well. Jesus' ministry is the Incarnation of this divine disposition: he heals on the Sabbath, eats with sinners, touches lepers — the God who sides with the vulnerable, present in human form.
20: Jethro's hospitality invitation — the priest of Midian recognizes the deliverer and brings him in. A shadow: Cornelius (Acts 10), the Roman centurion who receives the gospel first among the Gentiles, mirrors Jethro — a non-Israelite who receives the sent one with hospitality and whose household becomes the first Gentile church. The trajectory of the gospel — from Israel to the nations — is anticipated in Moses' sojourn in Midian and his reception into a Gentile priestly household.
21: Moses marries Zipporah — the deliverer takes a bride from among the nations. A theme: Christ takes a bride from among all nations — the church as the bride of Christ (Ephesians 5:25-27; Revelation 19:7-9). As Moses received Zipporah (a Midianite/Gentile) as his bride after fleeing Egypt, so Christ's bride is drawn from all peoples, languages, and nations (Revelation 5:9-10). The Gentile bride of the covenant mediator is a recurring type: Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba in Matthew's genealogy (Exodus 1:5-6), and Zipporah here.
22: 'I have been a stranger in a foreign land' — Gershom. The deliverer lives as an alien, belonging fully to no earthly nation. A shadow: 'The Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (Matthew 8:20). Jesus is the ultimate Gershom — the stranger in the world he made (John 1:10-11: 'the world did not recognize him... his own did not receive him'). The deliverer's estrangement from every earthly home is the condition that enables him to be the home of all who have no home.
23: The Israelites groan and cry out — the legal appeal (za'aqah) reaching the divine throne. A revelation of God that culminates in Christ: the God who hears the cry of the oppressed is the God who in Christ identifies himself as the one to whom the weary and heavy-laden should cry (Matthew 11:28). Jesus himself cries out from the cross ('Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani' — Mark 15:34), entering the position of the abandoned crier and transforming it from the inside. The God who hears the cry of Ex 2:23 becomes in Christ the one who cries it.
24: God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the covenant-memory as the engine of redemption. A direct theological foundation: Paul argues in Galatians 3:15-18 and Rom 4 that God's covenant with Abraham, confirmed 430 years before the law, is the ground of salvation — and this is the covenant YHWH remembers here. What God remembers in Ex 2:24 he fulfills in Christ: 'all the promises of God find their Yes in him' (2 Corinthians 1:20). The Exodus is the first installment of a redemption that finds its final expression in the cross and resurrection.
25: God saw Israel and was concerned — four acts of divine attention (heard, remembered, saw, knew) constitute the prelude to divine action. A revelation of God that culminates in the Incarnation: the God who sees, hears, remembers, and knows the condition of his people does not remain at a distance. In Christ, divine attention becomes divine presence: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14) — the God of Ex 2:25 comes himself to be with those whose condition he knows. The four verbs of attention become the one act of permanent arrival.
